KiteKit Scroll Stress Fixture

Use this page to compare full-page scrolling with a sticky header, then test the nested overflow area further down for trackpad and wheel input consistency.

Quick Checks

Scroll from top to bottom several times. Pause over the inner list and continue scrolling to see whether input handoff feels smooth or hesitant.

Tall document Sticky header Nested overflow Layered backgrounds

Nested Scroll Region

00:05
Initial pass through the nested scroller. Notice whether the inner region keeps pace with small wheel ticks.
00:14
Alternate fast and slow strokes to see whether momentum feels predictable.
00:21
Reverse direction mid-scroll and watch for hitching at the edge of the container.
00:36
Use short, repeated wheel input and confirm the inner region responds immediately.
00:47
Keep the pointer inside the panel and scroll until the container reaches its end.
00:58
Continue scrolling after the inner list ends and verify that control returns to the page cleanly.
01:10
Trackpad momentum should not stall when the content area is partially clipped.
01:22
Diagonal trackpad gestures should not misroute input or feel rubber-banded.
01:35
Scroll back upward through the container and look for frame pacing changes.
01:49
Repeat the same motion with the pointer just outside the nested region.
02:02
High-density text blocks below help reveal compositing or paint jitter.
02:17
One more pass through the nested region before continuing through the long page below.

Section 1

The first content block establishes a stable baseline. The card uses a translucent surface over a gradient background so small paint stalls are easier to spot during long wheel runs.

Keep scrolling while reading. Minor text and box-shadow movement tends to expose uneven frame cadence quickly.

Section 2

This section stretches the page further so momentum can build. Consistent deceleration should feel natural rather than stepping in discrete jumps.

If the page seems to fight the input device, repeat the same movement in the nested scroller above and compare the handoff behavior.

Section 3

Web views often feel worst when a sticky element remains fixed while the rest of the page moves beneath it. That is why the header stays pinned throughout this fixture.

Watch the border beneath the header. Jitter there is often easier to notice than in the larger body content.

Section 4

This card intentionally keeps the visual language simple. The goal is not synthetic benchmarking, only a repeatable human check for scroll and compositing quality.

Try both small wheel ticks and a continuous drag on a touchpad if your hardware supports it.

Section 5

Long-form reading layouts highlight whether the browser can hold a smooth pace through many consecutive paints. Subtle hitches become obvious after sustained movement.

If the app falls back to X11 inside Wayland, this part of the page is where many users will notice it first.

Section 6

Near the bottom of the fixture the document remains intentionally repetitive. That makes it easier to focus on feel rather than on changing content.

When you reach the end, reverse direction and run back to the top to see whether the experience remains consistent.
